exercises.title: Token bucket mechanics — predict available tokens
exercises.instructions:
A Bucket4j bucket: 100 tokens, refills 100/min (greedy).
Predict the state after each event:
1. Start: 100 tokens
2. 40 requests arrive: consume 40
3. 30 seconds pass (half minute)
4. 80 more requests arrive
5. 1 more request arrives
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Start: 100 tokens available

2. After 40 requests: 100 - 40 = 60 tokens remaining
   All 40 requests: ALLOWED

3. After 30 seconds (half minute refill - greedy):
   Greedy refill: adds tokens proportionally over time
   100 tokens/min → 50 tokens in 30 seconds
   60 + 50 = 110 → capped at 100 (bucket maximum)
   Tokens available: 100

4. After 80 more requests: 100 - 80 = 20 tokens remaining
   All 80 requests: ALLOWED

5. 1 more request: 20 - 1 = 19 tokens remaining
   Request: ALLOWED
   (Bucket has 19 tokens, request costs 1 → allowed)

Bucket would start returning 429 only when tokens reach 0.
With current state (19 tokens): still has capacity.

test_cases.expected:
1. Start: 100 tokens available

2. After 40 requests: 100 - 40 = 60 tokens remaining
   All 40 requests: ALLOWED

3. After 30 seconds (half minute refill - greedy):
   Greedy refill: adds tokens proportionally over time
   100 tokens/min → 50 tokens in 30 seconds
   60 + 50 = 110 → capped at 100 (bucket maximum)
   Tokens available: 100

4. After 80 more requests: 100 - 80 = 20 tokens remaining
   All 80 requests: ALLOWED

5. 1 more request: 20 - 1 = 19 tokens remaining
   Request: ALLOWED
   (Bucket has 19 tokens, request costs 1 → allowed)

Bucket would start returning 429 only when tokens reach 0.
With current state (19 tokens): still has capacity.

hints: Token bucket: tokens consumed per request, refilled over time
Greedy refill: proportional (50 tokens in 30s if rate is 100/min)
Bucket capped at max capacity (100)

exercises.title: Complete the Bucket4j rate limiter
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to create a 100 req/min bucket.

blanks.hint: Bandwidth.classic() = fixed capacity with refill rate
Refill.greedy() = distributes tokens evenly over time
attempts to consume tokens; returns false if bucket empty
HTTP status for Too Many Requests (RFC 6585)

hints: Bandwidth.classic: fixed max capacity with refill
Refill.greedy: smooth token distribution
tryConsume: non-blocking token consumption

exercises.title: Basic Bucket4j rate limiter
exercises.instructions:
Write a basic rate limiter service:
1. 100 requests per minute per key
2. RateLimiterService.isAllowed(String key) → boolean
3. Caffeine cache of buckets (per key)
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): first call allowed, drain bucket → 101st denied
hints: LoadingCache: creates bucket on first access per key (lazy init)
expireAfterAccess: clean up idle merchants from cache
unique test key: System.nanoTime() prevents test interference

exercises.title: 429 response with rate limit headers
exercises.instructions:
Write RateLimitResponseBuilder:
1. On rate limit exceeded: set response headers
2. X-RateLimit-Limit: 100
3. X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
4. Retry-After: 60 (seconds until refill)
5. Response body: {"error":"rate_limit_exceeded","retryAfter":60}
6. @SpringBootTest: headers and body correct
hints: 429: HTTP status code for Too Many Requests
Retry-After: seconds until client should retry (RFC 7231)
X-RateLimit-Reset: Unix timestamp when bucket refills (helps clients calculate wait)

exercises.title: OncePerRequestFilter rate limiting filter
exercises.instructions:
Write PaymentRateLimitFilter extending OncePerRequestFilter:
1. Extract merchantId from JWT Authorization header
2. If no auth: use IP address as key (for unauthenticated endpoints)
3. Check bucket: allowed → chain.doFilter, denied → 429
4. Add X-RateLimit-Remaining to all allowed responses
5. @SpringBootTest: filter instantiates correctly
hints: OncePerRequestFilter: guaranteed one execution per request (not duplicated)
X-Forwarded-For: real client IP behind load balancer/proxy
chain.doFilter: pass to next filter/controller — only call if allowed

exercises.title: Multiple rate limit tiers
exercises.instructions:
Implement tiered rate limits based on merchant plan:
1. STANDARD: 100 req/min
2. PREMIUM: 500 req/min
3. ENTERPRISE: 2000 req/min
4. TieredRateLimiter.isAllowed(String merchantId, String plan)
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): each tier has different capacity
hints: Plan enum: maps to token limit
merchantId+plan as cache key: HDFC_STANDARD vs HDFC_PREMIUM have separate buckets
Map.of: immutable map for plan → limit config

exercises.title: Idempotency key for payment deduplication
exercises.instructions:
Write IdempotencyService for payment API:
1. X-Idempotency-Key header: client sends unique key per payment attempt
2. Store processed payment IDs in Caffeine cache (24h TTL)
3. Duplicate request (same key): return cached result (not re-process)
4. New key: process and cache result
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): duplicate request returns same result, new key processes
hints: Idempotency key: UUID generated by client, same value on retry
24h TTL: safe window — client retries within 24h get same result
Optional.empty(): new key — process the payment normally

exercises.title: API key authentication filter
exercises.instructions:
Write ApiKeyAuthFilter (before rate limiting):
1. Extract X-API-Key header
2. Validate against a store (in-memory map for test)
3. Set merchantId in request attribute if valid
4. 401 if no API key, 403 if invalid
5. @SpringBootTest: valid key → merchantId set, invalid → 403
hints: req.setAttribute: share data between filters within same request
401 vs 403: 401 = no credentials (missing key), 403 = credentials present but invalid
In production: hash API keys in DB (never store plaintext)

exercises.title: Rate limit metrics with Micrometer
exercises.instructions:
Add Micrometer metrics to rate limiter:
1. Counter: rate.limit.allowed (tags: merchantId)
2. Counter: rate.limit.exceeded (tags: merchantId)
3. Gauge: rate.limit.tokens.remaining (current bucket level)
4. @SpringBootTest: metrics incremented on allow and deny
hints: Counter.builder: Micrometer counter (monotonically increasing)
tag('merchant', merchantId): LOW cardinality — only known merchants (not UUIDs)
Gauge: reads current value at scrape time (tokens remaining is a good gauge)

exercises.title: Distributed vs per-pod rate limiting failure
exercises.instructions:
10 Razorpay payment-service pods, each with in-memory Bucket4j bucket, limit 100 req/min per merchant.

Merchant 'MRC-HDFC-001' sends 950 requests/min, distributed by load balancer (round-robin).

1. How many requests reach each pod per minute?
2. How many requests are blocked per minute?
3. What is the actual throughput for the merchant?
4. What should have been the limit?
5. How does Redis-backed Bucket4j fix this?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Requests per pod per minute:
   950 req/min ÷ 10 pods = 95 req/min per pod
   Each pod's bucket: 100 tokens/min
   95 < 100 → NO requests blocked at ANY pod!

2. Requests blocked: 0 (zero!)
   Every pod sees only 95 req/min → all allowed
   Rate limiter completely ineffective.

3. Actual throughput: 950 req/min
   Merchant wanted 1000 req/min to flood the system
   With 10 pods × 100 limit → effectively 1000 req/min allowed
   Rate limit should have been 100 req/min total — is 950 req/min.

4. Should have been: 10 req/min per pod (if using per-pod buckets)
   100 total ÷ 10 pods = 10 tokens per pod
   But this breaks if pods scale dynamically (add pod → 110 total allowed)

5. Redis-backed Bucket4j fix:
   One shared bucket in Redis per merchantId
   All pods read/write the same Redis key atomically:
   Pod 1 consumes token → Redis count: 99 remaining
   Pod 2 consumes token → Redis count: 98 remaining
   ... after 100 total requests across ALL pods: 0 remaining
   Pod 3 gets request 101 → Redis says 0 → 429 returned
   Total limit: exactly 100/min regardless of pod count
   Pod scaling: adding pods doesn't change the rate limit

test_cases.expected:
1. Requests per pod per minute:
   950 req/min ÷ 10 pods = 95 req/min per pod
   Each pod's bucket: 100 tokens/min
   95 < 100 → NO requests blocked at ANY pod!

2. Requests blocked: 0 (zero!)
   Every pod sees only 95 req/min → all allowed
   Rate limiter completely ineffective.

3. Actual throughput: 950 req/min
   Merchant wanted 1000 req/min to flood the system
   With 10 pods × 100 limit → effectively 1000 req/min allowed
   Rate limit should have been 100 req/min total — is 950 req/min.

4. Should have been: 10 req/min per pod (if using per-pod buckets)
   100 total ÷ 10 pods = 10 tokens per pod
   But this breaks if pods scale dynamically (add pod → 110 total allowed)

5. Redis-backed Bucket4j fix:
   One shared bucket in Redis per merchantId
   All pods read/write the same Redis key atomically:
   Pod 1 consumes token → Redis count: 99 remaining
   Pod 2 consumes token → Redis count: 98 remaining
   ... after 100 total requests across ALL pods: 0 remaining
   Pod 3 gets request 101 → Redis says 0 → 429 returned
   Total limit: exactly 100/min regardless of pod count
   Pod scaling: adding pods doesn't change the rate limit

hints: Per-pod in-memory: limit multiplied by pod count (wrong!)
Redis atomic: all pods share same counter state
Dynamic scaling: Redis adapts automatically; per-pod doesn't

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay rate limiter with metrics
exercises.instructions:
Build complete rate limiter:
1. RateLimiterService: 100 req/min per merchant (Bucket4j)
2. RateLimitFilter: OncePerRequestFilter using the service
3. Micrometer counter: rate.limit.exceeded per merchant
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): allow, deny at 101, metric increments
5. All 3 pass
hints: Unique merchant keys with System.nanoTime(): prevents test cross-contamination
Counter registers on first call per tag combination
LoadingCache.get(): creates new bucket on first access per key

exercises.title: API key + rate limit integration
exercises.instructions:
Combine API key auth + rate limiting:
1. ApiKeyStore: validate key → return merchantId (or empty)
2. PaymentSecurityChain: validates key, then checks rate limit
3. If invalid key → 401; if rate limited → 429; else → process
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): valid key+ok rate→process, invalid key→401, valid key+rate exceeded→429
5. All 3 pass
hints: Result enum: clear return types for each security outcome
Auth before rate limit: validate identity first, then check quota
Separate keys per test: key-flipkart and key-zomato have independent buckets

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay API security gateway
exercises.instructions:
Build complete API security gateway:
1. ApiKeyAuthFilter: validates X-API-Key, sets merchantId attribute
2. RateLimitFilter: per-merchant bucket (Bucket4j), 100/min
3. IdempotencyService: dedup on X-Idempotency-Key
4. PaymentController: returns 200 with paymentId
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): valid flow, rate exceeded, duplicate idempotency key
6. All 3 pass
hints: Limit=5 in test: faster to drain bucket in test than 100
Idempotency: check AFTER rate limit (rate-limited requests don't consume idempotency slots)
DUPLICATE result: client should use cached paymentId — same as original response

exercises.title: Rate limit Prometheus alerting rules
exercises.instructions:
Write PromQL alerting rules for rate limiting:
1. Alert: RateLimitAbuseDetected — merchant exceeds 50 rate-limited requests in 5min
2. Alert: ApiKeyAbuse — 100+ invalid API key attempts from same IP in 1min
3. Explain: why Prometheus (not app code) for these alerts
4. @SpringBootTest: Counter increments correctly for the PromQL to work
exercises.starter_code: // Prometheus alerting rules + Spring Boot metric setup
hints: increase(): PromQL function — counts increase over time window
Prometheus aggregates ALL pods: accurate multi-pod rate limit monitoring
for: 1m: alert fires only if condition persists for 1 minute (reduces noise)

exercises.title: Complete M23 T5 rate limiting synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete M23 T5 synthesis:
1. RateLimiterService: 5/min per merchant (Bucket4j)
2. IdempotencyService: dedup with 24h cache
3. Micrometer counter: rate.limit.exceeded
4. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): allow, deny at 6th, idempotency dedup, metric correct
5. All 4 pass
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M23 T5 Rate Limiting synthesis
hints: Bucket limit=5 for test: fast to exhaust without 100 iterations
System.nanoTime() unique key: prevents test cross-contamination
idempotency: same result on both check() calls (deduplication works)
